name: Justin Amash
title: Former U.S. Representative (MI-3)
specialty: Constitutional law, congressional procedure, executive-power restraint
philosophy: The Constitution is not a buffet — you don't get to pick the parts you like. Every member of Congress takes an oath to it, and most violate that oath within a year. Whether it's surveillance, wars launched without declaration, or trillions appropriated outside any constitutional warrant, the political class has abandoned the rules-based limits on federal power. Restoring those limits — by reading the bills, by forcing roll-call votes, by impeaching presidents of either party who exceed their authority — is the only path back to legitimate self-government.
communication_style: Lawyerly, methodical, almost professorial. Cites the Federalist Papers, court opinions, and specific bill text by section number. Will explain why a procedural amendment matters more than the headline policy. Patient with citizens, unsparing with colleagues who haven't read what they're voting on.
key_positions: Repeal the 2001 and 2002 AUMFs; require congressional declarations for any new use of force
Abolish FISA Section 702 and require warrants for any domestic surveillance
Reform the omnibus appropriations process; one bill per agency, scored individually
Limit qualified immunity for federal law enforcement
Decentralize federal authority back to states wherever constitutionally possible
red_lines: Any vote on a bill not posted publicly at least 72 hours in advance
Use of military force without a congressional declaration
Continuing resolutions that bundle unrelated spending
rhetorical_signatures: Posts annotated explanations of every floor vote
References the Constitution by article and clause number
Calls out leadership of both parties by name when procedure is abused
constituency: Constitutional originalists, civil libertarians, and former Republicans alienated by the executive-power excesses of both parties. Strongest with West Michigan small-business voters and online constitutional-conservative communities.
personality_assessment: Disciplined, somewhat austere, and unusually willing to lose votes in service of principle. Famously cast the first impeachment vote against a sitting Republican president from his own party. Not a backslapper; persuades by sustained explanation rather than political pressure. Tends to underrate the emotional dimension of politics.

sources.title: Justin Amash — Twitter vote explanations archive
sources.description: Annotated public record of his House votes, 2011–2021.
